severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

hypertrophy_hepatocellular.conditions: Assumes adaptive enzyme induction/organellar proliferation and removal of stimulus. If accompanied by necrosis, cholestasis, or persistent enzyme leakage, use lesion-specific slower timeline.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

necrosis_hepatocellular.conditions: Only if injury is focal/zonal and lobular framework remains intact. Massive/confluent necrosis or collapse can lead to fibrosis and non-recovery.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

vacuolation_hepatocellular.conditions: Simple glycogen/hydropic/macrovesicular change usually resolves quickly; phospholipidosis/microvesicular toxic change can persist several weeks.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

bile_duct_hyperplasia.conditions: Best recovery when secondary to reversible cholestatic/enzyme-induction signals and without established fibrosis; slower if chronic cholangitic/fibrotic component present.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

inflammation_portal_lobular.conditions: Uncomplicated cellular infiltrates may resolve in weeks; necroinflammatory and cholestatic injury recover more slowly.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

kupffer_cell_hypertrophy_hyperplasia.conditions: Usually parallels clearance of particulates, hemolytic products, or phospholipid/lipid burden; may persist longer if pigment/storage material remains.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

glycogen_depletion.conditions: Usually reverses rapidly with restoration of intake/metabolic state; prolonged only when coupled to ongoing systemic toxicity.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

tubular_degeneration_necrosis.conditions: Rat mild acute tubular injury can show morphologic repair within days; marked necrosis with basement membrane destruction can leave permanent scarring.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

tubular_basophilia.conditions: Interpret as reparative regeneration after prior injury. Persistence beyond 8 weeks suggests ongoing injury or chronicity/CPN confounding.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

tubular_dilatation.conditions: Reversible when secondary to transient obstruction/injury and preserved nephron architecture; persistent when tied to chronic atrophy/fibrosis.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

interstitial_inflammation.conditions: Best for mild, nonfibrosing infiltrates; slower/poorer when fibrosis accompanies nephritis.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

interstitial_nephritis.conditions: Depends on extent of tubular loss and fibrosis. Mild drug-associated nephritis may improve; chronic fibrosing nephritis often persists.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

mineralization.conditions: Dystrophic/nephrocalcific mineralization is often persistent; use 'none' if established mineral with fibrosis/architectural distortion is present.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

cast_formation.conditions: If secondary to transient proteinuria/debris load and renal function recovers; longer if ongoing tubular injury persists.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

follicular_cell_hypertrophy.conditions: Best supported for enzyme-induction/TSH-mediated adaptive change after removal of stimulus. Rat is more sensitive and often faster than dog/NHP.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

follicular_cell_hyperplasia.conditions: Hyperplasia usually resolves more slowly than pure hypertrophy and may persist beyond 2 weeks in stronger thyroid-axis perturbation.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

colloid_alteration.conditions: Usually tracks normalization of thyroid-axis signaling and can improve faster than cellular hyperplasia.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

cortical_hypertrophy.conditions: Most defensible for stress/adaptive steroidogenic drive. If accompanied by necrosis or hemorrhage, use lesion-specific rules.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

cortical_vacuolation.conditions: Lipid depletion/accumulation and adaptive vacuolation generally recover when endocrine drive normalizes; phospholipid-type vacuolation can persist longer.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

medullary_hyperplasia.conditions: Limited generic evidence; use cautiously and prefer case-specific interpretation.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

extramedullary_hematopoiesis_increase.conditions: Reactive EMH usually regresses quickly once marrow demand normalizes; persistent only if ongoing hematopoietic stress remains.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

lymphoid_depletion.conditions: Common stress/immunomodulatory lesion; usually recovers over weeks after stimulus removal.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

congestion.conditions: Hemodynamic congestion should reverse rapidly if cause resolves and no secondary tissue injury is present.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

hemosiderosis.conditions: Pigment clearance is slower than resolution of the initiating hematologic lesion and may remain above background for weeks to months.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

cortical_atrophy_lymphoid_depletion.conditions: Best supported for stress/glucocorticoid-mediated involution; younger rodents recover faster than larger species/older animals.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

apoptosis_increased.conditions: If due to acute stress/steroid effect and not ongoing cytotoxic injury.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

decreased_spermatogenesis.conditions: Assumes surviving stem spermatogonia and withdrawal of insult; at least one spermatogenic cycle is usually required. Dogs and NHPs are slower.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

germ_cell_degeneration.conditions: Recovery faster when injury is limited to meiotic/post-meiotic cells; slower/poorer when spermatogonial stem cells or Sertoli cells are affected.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

seminiferous_tubule_atrophy.conditions: Diffuse tubular atrophy often indicates stem-cell/Sertoli-cell loss and may not fully recover. Focal/segmental atrophy can be less prognostically severe.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

leydig_cell_hypertrophy.conditions: Adaptive endocrine-driven Leydig changes can improve over weeks if axis normalizes.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

hypocellularity.conditions: Quiescent/pharmacologic marrow suppression is often reversible; apoptotic ablation, stromal injury, or myelofibrosis recover more slowly or incompletely.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

myeloid_depletion.conditions: Neutrophil lineage typically recovers faster than erythroid mass because turnover is faster.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

erythroid_depletion.conditions: Marrow production can recover quickly, but blood red-cell normalization lags because circulating RBC lifespan is long.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

cellularity_increase.conditions: Reactive hypercellularity/lineage rebound usually contracts once the driver resolves.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

mucosal_hyperplasia_glandular.conditions: Reactive hyperplasia resolves if the irritant or trophic stimulus is removed and dysplasia/neoplasia is absent.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

mucosal_hyperplasia_forestomach.conditions: Forestomach-specific; not applicable to species lacking forestomach.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

erosion.conditions: Superficial mucosal injury heals relatively quickly if irritation ceases and ulceration/fibrosis do not develop.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

ulceration.conditions: Deeper ulcers can heal within weeks but may leave fibrosis/scarring; use slower end if perforation/chronic inflammation present.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

cardiomyocyte_degeneration_necrosis.conditions: True cardiomyocyte necrosis is permanent cell loss. Functional compensation may occur, but histologic normalization does not.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

inflammation.conditions: Limited recovery only for mild interstitial inflammation without significant myocyte loss.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

fibrosis.conditions: Established fibrosis is considered irreversible for this lookup.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

alveolar_macrophage_accumulation.conditions: Fast early improvement can occur, but complete resolution may take weeks, especially with phospholipidosis or particulate burden.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

inflammation.conditions: Uncomplicated inflammatory infiltrates may resolve; fibrosing/chronic granulomatous processes are slower or non-reversible.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

alveolar_epithelial_hyperplasia.conditions: Reactive hyperplasia may regress if injury ceases; persistence raises concern for chronic remodeling/fibrosis.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

hyperplasia_follicular_paracortical.conditions: Reactive draining-node hyperplasia generally recedes over weeks once antigenic stimulus/dose-site inflammation resolves.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

sinus_histiocytosis.conditions: Usually resolves when particulate/material burden clears; longer if persistent foreign material remains.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

atrophy.conditions: Best supported for stress/immunosuppressive atrophy that stops with withdrawal of driver.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

inflammation.conditions: Typical local reactogenicity or injection trauma improves within days to weeks; 2-4 week recovery groups usually suffice to show reversal.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

necrosis.conditions: Requires regeneration without deep tissue loss; fibrosis commonly remains after healing.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

fibrosis.conditions: Scar tissue may mature but not return to control-normal histology; treat as non-reversible for verdicting.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

granuloma.conditions: Foreign-material/adjuvant granulomas may persist for months to a year; short-lived sterile granulomas can improve more quickly.

species_specific_modifiers.general_recommendation: Use organ-process-specific modifiers rather than one global species multiplier.
notes: Rat liver repair is often faster than dog/NHP because rodent hepatocellular regeneration is rapid and well documented experimentally.
Testicular recovery tracks spermatogenic cycle length and stem-cell survival; dog and NHP are slower than rat/mouse.
Rat/mouse thymus and marrow typically rebound faster than large animals, especially for stress and cytostatic effects.
For kidney, species differences are less about intrinsic repair speed than about background lesions and interpretive confounding (e.g., rat CPN).

rationale: A fixed ±2 week band distorts short lesions and understates long lesions.
Recovery is biologically asymmetric: slower-than-expected recovery is more common than faster-than-expected recovery.
Confidence-dependent bands reflect whether a value is supported by direct recovery data versus extrapolation from tissue biology.
uncertainty_model.implementation_note: For entries with an explicit low/high range in the table, display that range directly. Use the uncertainty model only when converting a single-point estimate or when widening the range for low-confidence extrapolated rows.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

liver_weight_increase.conditions: Use only when correlated with adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy. If weight increase tracks fatty change, cholestasis, or necrosis, use the structural lesion instead.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

thymus_weight_decrease.conditions: Usually parallels stress-mediated thymic involution and normalizes rapidly after stress/drug withdrawal.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

testis_weight_decrease.conditions: Weight recovery lags histologic improvement and depends on whether spermatogenesis resumes; no recovery if diffuse tubular atrophy persists.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

ALT_AST_increase.conditions: Serum leakage markers can normalize faster than complete histologic repair if injury stops; persistent elevation suggests ongoing necroinflammation.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

bilirubin_increase.conditions: Depends on cholestasis/hemolysis resolution; often slower than aminotransferases.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

BUN_creatinine_increase.conditions: Functional recovery may precede or lag morphology depending on nephron reserve and severity of acute tubular injury.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

neutrophil_wbc_decrease.conditions: Myeloid counts usually recover within days to weeks if marrow progenitors/stroma are preserved.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

platelet_decrease.conditions: Platelet recovery is relatively fast because platelet lifespan is only days; use slower end in dog/NHP or when marrow lesion is severe.

severity_modulation.note: Use only when lesion remains in same biological class; if marked/severe implies necrosis, fibrosis, stem-cell loss, or architecture collapse, reclassify lesion rather than multiply time.

rbc_hgb_hct_decrease.conditions: Production may recover earlier, but normalization of circulating RBC mass is constrained by RBC lifespan; dogs are slower than rats.

severity_modulation.note: No defensible generic time-scaling; severity affects confidence/adversity more than recovery duration.

reticulocyte_increase.conditions: Reticulocytosis often normalizes rapidly once the regenerative drive resolves.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

body_weight_decrease.conditions: Fastest when due to palatability/stress; slower when driven by organ toxicity or endocrine disruption.

severity_modulation.note: Marked/severe lesions often reflect a change in biology (e.g., scaffold loss, fibrosis, diffuse atrophy) rather than a longer version of the same recoverable lesion.

PT_APTT_fibrinogen_change.conditions: Coagulation proteins turn over in days, so isolated coagulation abnormalities often normalize quickly if hepatic synthesis and inflammation recover.

validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.adjustment_needed: Replace with organ-specific values; thyroid/liver shorter, stomach can be similar
Current value systematically high for many adaptive lesions

validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.literature_supported_weeks: 0.5-2 renal osmotic; 1-4 hepatic simple vacuolation; 2-8 phospholipidosis
validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.adjustment_needed: Split by mechanism rather than one bucket
Reasonable for renal regeneration only; should not be organ-agnostic

validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.literature_supported_weeks: 1-8 liver/kidney if reparable; none in heart

validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.adjustment_needed: Too long as default, but can be too short when chronic
Current ballpark acceptable but still needs organ/context awareness

validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.literature_supported_weeks: 6-10 rat; 8-12 dog if stem cells survive; none if diffuse atrophy
validation_of_prior_hardcoded_values.adjustment_needed: Too long for reversible endocrine/germ-cell suppression, too short for nonrecovering atrophy

why: Severity grades quantify extent/complexity of morphologic change; they are not validated linear predictors of time-to-recovery.
Across organs, marked severity often changes the biology (e.g., stem-cell loss, fibrosis, scaffold loss, permanent cardiomyocyte loss) rather than merely extending recovery time.
Therefore a single 1.0x/1.5x/2.0x or 2.5x scheme is not defensible.
recommended_rules: Adaptive hypertrophy/hyperplasia: allow only modest scaling (max ~1.5x) for moderate/marked lesions if the lesion remains clearly adaptive and recoverable.
Degenerative/necrotic lesions: use a threshold model. Mild-to-moderate lesions may recover; marked/severe lesions often switch to 'unlikely' or 'none' rather than 2.0x-2.5x.
Heart necrosis/fibrosis: no multiplier; treat as non-reversible.
Testis: severity interacts with lesion compartment. Germ-cell-only lesions can recover over 1 cycle; diffuse tubular atrophy/stem-cell loss should move to unlikely/none.
Kidney: mild basophilic regeneration is a healing lesion, not a longer-duration injury. Marked tubular injury with fibrosis should switch class.

evidence_log.source: Hall et al. 2012, Toxicol Pathol, 'Liver hypertrophy: a review of adaptive (adverse and non-adverse) changes'
evidence_log.statement: Hepatomegaly as a consequence of hepatocellular hypertrophy without histologic or clinical pathology evidence of liver toxicity is considered adaptive and non-adverse.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports expected reversibility for adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy after removal of stimulus.

evidence_log.source: Michalopoulos & DeFrances 1997, Science, 'Liver regeneration'; Mehendale 2005, Toxicol Pathol, 'Tissue repair...'; Anand et al. 2003, Toxicology
evidence_log.statement: Rat liver repair is rapid after sublethal toxic injury; in one chloroform model injury peaked at 24 h and returned to normal after 48 h, while delayed repair at higher injury burdens led to progression and death.
evidence_log.key_use: Anchors rat-faster-than-dog liver recovery and supports threshold rather than linear severity scaling.

evidence_log.source: NTP Nonneoplastic Lesion Atlas 2024, Liver - Fatty Change
evidence_log.statement: Macrovesicular fatty change is generally readily reversible; microvesicular change more often reflects toxicity and can be slower/less predictable.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports short recovery windows for simple vacuolation/glycogen-type change, with longer windows for toxic microvesicular injury/phospholipidosis.

evidence_log.source: Ettlin et al. 2010, Toxicologic Pathology, 'Successful Drug Development Despite Adverse Preclinical Findings Part 2'; Greaves 2012 Respiratory Tract chapter
evidence_log.statement: Drug-induced phospholipidosis generally resolves after withdrawal but may take considerable time; pulmonary phospholipidosis changes are still visible several weeks after withdrawal across multiple species.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports longer 'possible' recovery windows for phospholipid-type vacuolation/macrophage accumulation than for simple hydropic/glycogen change.

evidence_log.source: Reasor 1984, Am J Pathol / PubMed snippet; McCloud et al. 1995, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol / PubMed snippet
evidence_log.statement: Rat alveolar macrophage phospholipidosis is reversible after termination of treatment; after 7 days chlorphentermine treatment there was marked reduction in cellular changes after 4 days recovery, though lamellar bodies persisted.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports fast early functional/morphologic improvement but slower complete resolution in lung macrophage accumulation.

evidence_log.source: Huisinga et al. 2020, Toxicol Pathol, thyroid FCHH workshop; Yano et al. 2025, Food Chem Toxicol snippet; Canzler et al. 2025, Arch Toxicol snippet
evidence_log.statement: Follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia are common nonclinical findings; rat recovery examples show substantial reversion within 14 days in some enzyme-induction settings, but increased thyroid weight can still persist after 2 weeks in stronger direct/indirect thyroid toxicant models.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports rat thyroid adaptive windows centered on 2-6 weeks and slower windows for hyperplasia than for pure hypertrophy/colloid depletion.

evidence_log.source: NTP Nonneoplastic Lesion Atlas 2024, Kidney - Renal Tubule Regeneration
evidence_log.statement: Renal tubular regeneration occurs as a reparative response to previous degeneration/necrosis and is characterized by basophilia, karyomegaly and nuclear crowding.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports interpreting tubular basophilia as a healing lesion rather than a primary chronic lesion.

evidence_log.source: Cuppage & Tate 1967, Am J Pathol; Haagsma et al. 1980, Br J Exp Pathol snippets
evidence_log.statement: After mercuric chloride injury in rat kidney, tubular epithelium was reconstituted between days 2 and 5; permanent patchy fibrotic lesions can occur after more severe injury.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports very fast recovery for mild acute tubular injury in rats and a severity threshold beyond which chronic residual lesions remain.

evidence_log.source: Kiss et al. 2016, Histopathological Evaluation of Contrast-Induced AKI Rodent Models
evidence_log.statement: Tubular vacuolization is often reversible even after very high contrast media doses; in one rat study the vacuolization almost disappeared 48 hours after iobitridol.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports short windows for osmotic/hydropic vacuolation distinct from necrosis.

evidence_log.source: Mengs et al. 2004, 13-week senna rat study with 8-week recovery
evidence_log.statement: After 8 weeks recovery, histopathologic kidney abnormalities reversed except for residual brown pigment.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports separating reparative basophilia (recoverable) from pigment (can persist).

evidence_log.source: Everds et al. 2013, Toxicol Pathol, stress responses review
evidence_log.statement: Routine study stress can produce thymic/splenic lymphoid depletion, altered thymus/spleen/adrenal weights, and altered leukocyte counts.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports rapid reversibility expectations for stress-mediated lymphoid/adrenal findings once stimulus ends.

evidence_log.source: Branceni et al. 1966, thymic involution and recovery in prednisolone-treated neonatal rats
evidence_log.statement: After steroid-induced thymic involution in rats, thymic mass, thymocytes and peripheral lymphocytes recovered fully by 3 weeks.
evidence_log.key_use: Anchors rat thymic recovery windows around 1-3 weeks for stress/steroid-type involution.

evidence_log.source: Zhang et al. 2020, Sepin-1 28-day rat toxicity study with 28-day recovery
evidence_log.statement: Minimal-to-moderate splenic extramedullary hematopoiesis, minimal splenic lymphoid depletion, minimal-to-mild thymic lymphoid depletion, and minimal-to-mild lymph node hyperplasia were mostly reversible after a 28-day recovery period.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports 2-4 week windows for reactive hematolymphoid findings in rats.

evidence_log.source: Miyata et al. 2009, Evaluation of Myelotoxicity in Dietary Restricted Rats
evidence_log.statement: After 14 days dosing and 7 days recovery, reticulocyte and platelet rebound plus splenic EMH were observed in 5-FU-treated rats, indicating marrow recovery dynamics within about 1 week.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports short marrow rebound windows for proliferative hematopoietic tissues in rats.

evidence_log.source: Reagan et al. 2011 best practices for bone marrow; Hu et al. 2016 palbociclib; Graziano et al. 1997 CI-994
evidence_log.statement: Bone marrow suppression/hypocellularity can be reversible after withdrawal; in one rat/dog toxicity program all non-testicular changes reversed within 4 weeks in rats and 9 weeks in dogs.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports species-dependent rat-faster-than-dog modifiers for marrow lesions.

evidence_log.source: Vidal et al. 2015, Morphologic manifestations of testicular and epididymal toxicity; Soares et al. 2009 dog spermatogenic cycle
evidence_log.statement: Many spermatogonial injuries can be reversible if surviving stem spermatogonia remain; recovery is constrained by the long duration of spermatogenesis. Total spermatogenesis duration is ~40-54 days in most mammals and toward the upper end in dogs.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports lesion windows tied to at least one spermatogenic cycle and slower dog/NHP recovery.

evidence_log.source: Campion et al. 2012 male reprotoxicity review
evidence_log.statement: In a Leydig-cell toxicant model, signs of recovering spermatogenesis were present by 3 weeks post-exposure and mature Leydig cells by 5 weeks.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports rat recovery windows of several weeks, not days, for endocrine/testicular lesions.

evidence_log.source: Graziano et al. 1997, CI-994 rat/dog toxicity
evidence_log.statement: Testicular degeneration in rats and dogs was the notable lesion that did not fully reverse within 4-week rat and 9-week dog recovery periods, whereas other changes did.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports classifying marked testicular lesions/atrophy as slower or potentially non-recovering.

evidence_log.source: Nolte et al. 2016 GI INHAND; Greaves 2012 digestive system chapter
evidence_log.statement: Florid nonglandular stomach hyperplasia can be completely reversible following withdrawal of the inciting stimulus; in an omeprazole model functional and morphologic gastric changes after 60 days treatment were fully reversible after 42 days withdrawal.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports expected reversibility for reactive hyperplasia and multi-week healing for deeper ulcerative change.

evidence_log.source: Berridge et al. 2016 INHAND cardiovascular lesions
evidence_log.statement: Cardiomyocytes are terminally differentiated; cardiomyocyte necrosis represents permanent loss of contractile capability.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports 'none' or very limited reversibility for true cardiomyocyte necrosis and fibrosis regardless of grade.

evidence_log.source: Baldrick 2016 vaccine dose-site reactions; Green 2016 Preclinical Toxicology of Vaccines
evidence_log.statement: A non-dose recovery period of 2-4 weeks was sufficient to show reversibility of typical vaccine dose-site effects. Simple injection trauma shows reduced inflammation and healing with regeneration/fibrosis by day 10, but alum-associated macrophage aggregates/focal fibrosis may persist for months to 1 year.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports short windows for ordinary injection-site inflammation/necrosis, but long persistence for adjuvant granulomatous change and fibrosis.

evidence_log.source: Baldrick 2016; vaccine-related lymphoid findings
evidence_log.statement: Reactive draining lymph node and splenic changes accompanying vaccination generally reverse within a 2-4 week recovery interval.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports expected reversibility for reactive lymph node hyperplasia/histiocytosis when inciting antigen/material is cleared.

evidence_log.source: Baldrick 2016 / Green 2016
evidence_log.statement: Organ-weight recovery generally parallels the underlying lesion: thymus/spleen effects with immune/stress responses recover over weeks, while dose-site granulomatous changes can persist much longer.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports deriving organ-weight timelines from pathology rather than creating separate organ-agnostic constants.

evidence_log.source: Derelanko 1987 rat RBC lifespan; An 2016 physiology model; Heilmann 1993 dog platelet lifespan
evidence_log.statement: Rat RBC lifespan is ~55-66 days; dog platelet lifespan is ~6 days. These turnover differences support much faster platelet/reticulocyte normalization than RBC mass normalization, and slower dog than rat RBC recovery.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports continuous endpoint timelines for hematology and species modifiers.

evidence_log.source: OECD TG 407 (2008); OECD TG 408; EPA OPPTS 870.3100; ICH M3(R2)
evidence_log.statement: OECD 407 uses at least 14 days follow-up after 28-day dosing; OECD 408 uses an appropriate recovery period after 90-day dosing; EPA 90-day oral toxicity guidance allows a satellite group observed for reversibility/persistence; ICH M3(R2) frames reversibility as a goal of repeated-dose safety assessment.
evidence_log.key_use: Shows that common recovery-group durations are design conventions, not lesion-specific biology, and therefore should not be reused as lookup constants.

evidence_log.source: Schafer et al. 2018 severity grades; Miller 2017 cell injury review; NTP degeneration atlas
evidence_log.statement: Severity grades describe extent/complexity of morphologic change; severe or prolonged injury can pass a 'point of no return' into irreversible necrosis. No source supports a universal linear week-multiplier across organs.
evidence_log.key_use: Supports replacing flat multipliers with lesion-specific threshold rules.

metadata.evidence_philosophy: Values are evidence-informed synthesis ranges, not universal biological constants. Direct recovery-study timepoints are rare; many rows are derived from a mixture of direct recovery examples, consensus pathology reviews, and tissue-turnover biology.
important_limitations: Many pathology references describe reversibility qualitatively without publishing exact week ranges for every lesion-organ-species combination.
Study design recovery intervals (e.g., 2 or 4 weeks) are not themselves proof that a lesion necessarily recovers within that window.
Compound mechanism, dose, duration, age, background lesions, and persistence of stored material can dominate the generic timeline.
recommended_engine_policy: Prefer organ-specific lesion rows over global finding-type defaults.
If a lesion has confidence='low' and no matching direct recovery example, widen the displayed uncertainty band and avoid hard 'persistent' verdicts.
Escalate to ASSESSMENT_LIMITED_BY_DURATION when observed recovery time is shorter than the literature-supported low end for that lesion/species/severity context.
